text: You can begin by seeing the left vagus nerve traversing through the thoracic cavity going along the esophagus.
The head is to the top, the feet are to the bottom, the diaphragm is the bottom left view.
The inferior pulmonary ligament can easily be seen in the center of the video, and the heart beating with the left phrenic nerve can easily be seen.
As you proceed to move some of the pericardial fat, the left phrenic nerve can be seen even better.

We begin by using the bipolar ligature device to take down the inferior pulmonary ligament using a grasper or a Frasier ringed forcep to retract the lung superiorly with three thoracoscopic ports used for the surgery.
We use the ligature device to divide the inferior pulmonary ligament, sweeping the tissue superiorly up towards the head of the patient.

This is between the aorta and the pericardium of the heart, taking care not to go up too far and damage the inferior pulmonary vein, which is at the most superior aspect of the inferior pulmonary ligament.
We use the device to dissect into this region and expose the esophagus.
The left vagus nerve can easily be seen traversing away just inferior to the ligature device going down towards the abdomen.
We use the gentle pushing and spreading technique to evaluate the pleura and move it away from the area.
We then switch to a hook device.
Sometimes we use cautery with the hook when we are far away from mucosa, and at other times when we are closer to the mucosa, we only use the hook without cautery.
As we begin to hook on the muscular layer of the esophagus, we can begin to see the diverticulum as it points towards the left side of the patient.
We use the hook to divide without the use of cautery or electricity to make sure that we do not inadvertently damage the mucosal surface of the esophagus.
By using the hook, we are dividing the inner circular and outer longitudinal layers of the muscular wall of the esophagus.
Since this is a diverticulum of pulsion, it is not completely covered by muscle.
It was easy to identify and dividing the muscle both superiorly and inferiorly to the diverticulum is quite easy to do.
We then grasp the very tip of the diverticulum using a node grasper made by Scanlon.
We encircle the diverticulum along its base to make sure that we have dissected all of the muscle away from the base of the diverticulum.
This is an important maneuver that must be done in order to completely remove the diverticulum and prevent future recurrence.

This muscular division will soon be closed after we have stapled across the bottom of the diverticulum, but first must be opened and splayed to allow adequate visualization of the base of the diverticulum.

It is important during some portion of the procedure that a bougie is placed through the lumen of the esophagus with visualization taking care not to have the bougie exit through the diverticulum or through any point of resistance.
Clear communication between anesthesia and surgery or having the bougie placed by the surgeon is highly recommended to prevent inadvertent injury.

As we begin to divide the muscular layers around the base of the diverticulum, you can see we then use a peanut dissector to push all of the remaining muscular tissue away from the base of the diverticulum.
By grasping on the diverticulum with a node grasper, we can move it from side to side and see both sides of the base.
Sometimes if the diverticulum is thicker we use a purple load stapler and in this particular circumstance we chose to use a thinner tan load endogia stapler.
The Tanload EndoGIA stapler is for a smaller, thinner walled diverticulum that may require a tighter seal.
It has graded staples that are tight on the inside and larger on the outside so that good perfusion to the distal tip of the staple line is achieved.
During placement of this stapler, it is important to note that a bougie is in place.
A 53 bougie is placed inside the esophagus and used to prevent narrowing during stapling.
We then use the note grasper to divide any of the tissue that is around the base of the esophagus so that we can rotate it.

It is important to first close the muscle over the staple line of the diverticulum and then to place two traction sutures such that you can rotate the esophagus over and perform a contralateral myotomy.
We placed a penrose around the base of the esophagus to help elevate it and complete our dissection.
We took care to identify and dissect out the right and left vagal nerves so that they were not damaged during this dissection.
It is important to note also that when we lift up on the penrose, you can clearly see the indwelling bougie that was left in place.
To close the myotomy that was done only to expose the base of the diverticulum, we use a V-lock suture.
This is a barbed catching suture that is placed around a figure eight initial stitch that is then passed through a small loop that is in the distal tip of the suture.
This allows for a proximal seal and the barbed suture prevents it from unraveling.
This facilitates the case, allows it to happen faster and creates a nice closure of the myotomy.

As we begin to close the myotomy over the diverticular staple line, you can see that all of the layers of the pleura and the muscle are closed for a very clear closure over the diverticular staple line.

Once we have completely closed all of the muscle over the esophagus where the diverticulaectomy occurred, we will then place stay sutures superiorly and inferiorly on the anterior wall of the esophagus to retract it posteriorly so that a contralateral myotomy can occur.
It is important not to allow the V-lock suture to get looped during placement so that a tight closure can be created, such as you see in this particular case.
It is important also to constantly monitor to make sure that the patient is tolerating this exposure.
In some cases, CO2 insufflation can help visualize the base of the esophagus and can help facilitate the procedure.
Although not always do we find that we have to use CO2, it does sometimes help.
As we begin to close over this final layer, you can see that we begin locking the V-lock suture so that it does not become unraveled using another layer to tightly close over this myotomy.
It is important to make sure that during this part of the procedure you are not inadvertently strangulating any unnecessary tissue.
A two-layered closure running with the V-lock allows for a tight closure and subsequent attention can be paid to the contralateral side for the myotomy.
It is important to note also that through one of the ports, a ring grasper is placed to hold the lung superiorly towards the head of the patient.
For orientation of this video, the aorta or the posterior side of the thoracoscopic view is to the right.
The feet are inferiorly, the head is superiorly, and the heart and anterior portion of the chest are to the left.

after we've composed a double layer v-lock closed suture to close the myotomy in two separate layers we can then begin to cut the suture and address the other contralateral myotomy we continue to dissect the remaining tissue around the esophagus to facilitate mobilization you could either place stay sutures anteriorly rotating it to the right

or the patient's left, or you can place stay sutures just to the patient's left side of the myotomy, as we have done in this case, rolling the esophagus over towards the heart, rolling it in the patient's orientation clockwise or in the surgeon's orientation counterclockwise or to the right side of the patient, the left side of the field.
By doing this we are able to identify that we are on the contralateral side from the myotomy as you can see some of the remaining V-lock sutures to the left side of the esophagus.
With the bougie in place we then perform a hook cautery myotomy where you can see we're now dividing the circular layer of the inner muscular circular layer of the esophagus muscle.
We use the hook cautery to divide these inner circular muscular fibers by hooking on small fibers at a time and then pulling and retracting those muscle fibers until they fall apart.
By keeping the bougie in place, we are easily able to see the lumen of the esophagus and stay away from the mucosal layer.
The pin rose is no longer needed but may occasionally help in retraction of the esophagus when necessary.
It is important to note that any bleeding that has ensued can be treated with a small sponge soaked in epinephrine to cause vasoconstriction of the vessels during the dissection.
As you start to go down towards the inferior aspect of the esophagus, you can see a change in the direction of the muscle fibers.
This change in direction of muscle fibers represents the sling fibers or the gastroesophageal junction.
If you plan on performing a myotomy such as a Belzi, you can extend this myotomy approximately two centimeters beyond the esophagogastric junction.
However, if you are not planning on performing a myotomy, you would only want to extend this myotomy down to those fibers for approximately one centimeter.
In this case, we did not do an anti-reflux procedure and therefore we extended our myotomy only down onto one centimeter of the esophagogastric junction or beyond the esophagogastric junction.

The left vagus nerve can be seen traversing across the myotomy and the myotomy can be seen as if it was complete and thorough and covered the entire length of the diverticulum all the way down to the esophagogastric junction.
The esophagus and epiphrenic diverticulum and diverticulectomy are all clearly depicted with the anatomy labeled in the center screen image.
